unitTitle: Y7 Clear messaging in digital media (Teach Computing — adapted)

lessons.title: Get the message across — search, screenshot, good posters
lessons.objectives:
I can choose search terms relating to a topic
I can use tools to screenshot an image into another application
I can identify key features of a good poster
lessons.outline:
Routine first (same every lesson). Vocabulary on board: search term · screenshot · annotate. TA: everyone has a browser + slides/doc open. Read the 3 “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–5) Starter — the “one way” road sign (think–pair–share). What is the message? How do you know? Pupils CHOOSE on the worksheet (no writing).
   • Support: pick the message from 3 choices.  • Core: name a place they have seen public messaging.  • Challenge: why a picture beats a long sentence.
2. (5–15) What makes a good poster? (I do). Build a class list — clear message, matching picture, easy-to-read text. Pupils TICK ALL good features (multi-select). Keep the list for Lesson 2.
3. (15–25) Search for an online safety poster (we do). Choose search terms; search; pick one good poster. Likely error: vague terms (“stuff”) → fix-words “say WHAT and WHERE — online safety poster.”  • Support: word-bank of terms.  • Core: write 3 terms + why.  • Challenge: refine terms when a search fails. Play the search-image video as a teacher hook.
4. (25–45) You-do — screenshot the poster, paste it into slides/a doc, ADD LABELS on the good features. Play the screenshot video as a hook.  • Support: printed “press these keys” card.  • Core: label features.  • Challenge: label AND say why each helps. TA: “prompt, do not do it for them.”
5. (45–50) Plenary — self-tick the 3 “I can…”; show the labelled screenshot.
Differentiation on the SAME task, low floor / high ceiling — nobody’s screen looks “less done”. Videos have motion/sound — teacher-played, the teacher’s choice; otherwise no flashing/sound on slides.

lessons.title: Poster making — plan, image, create
lessons.objectives:
I can plan a poster to clearly convey a message
I can choose and download a suitable image
I can create a poster using a publishing application
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on board: portrait · landscape · download · heading · body text. TA: publishing app (e.g. Canva) + a policy-checked image site open; the L1 “good poster” list on the board. Read the 3 “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–5) Starter — the Mobile Threats poster: good or not so good? Pupils TICK ALL the good features (multi-select); design is personal so answers vary if explained.
   • Support: choose the poster’s main message.  • Core: name one thing that could be better.  • Challenge: why the light→dark background helps the reader.
2. (5–20) Plan first (I do). Model main message → main colour → portrait/landscape → image theme on the activity worksheet. Stress: everything serves the message.
3. (20–30) Choosing colours (we do). Use a colour tool for companion colours; black/white text is a safe default. Pupils note colour codes.
4. (30–40) You-do — find and DOWNLOAD a licensed image that matches the theme; start building the poster. Play the paste-an-image video as a hook. Likely error: re-uses the chosen poster’s image → fix-words “find your OWN licensed image.”  • Support: “black/white text” default.  • Core: portrait/landscape with a reason.  • Challenge: check the image licence; pick two companion colours.
5. (40–45) Plenary — self-tick the 3 “I can…”; screenshot the poster so far. Keep the plan for Lesson 3.
Differentiation on the SAME task; minimal writing (tick/choose/screenshot); no flashing/sound on slides (the image video is teacher-played).

lessons.title: Brand — recolour a logo, build a slide, give feedback
lessons.objectives:
I can modify a logo using a graphic editing program
I can choose how to combine text and graphics on a slide
I can use digital tools to provide feedback on design choices
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on board: brand · logo · slide · comment. TA: a paint/graphics app + slides software with comments; the three logo files where pupils can open them. Read the 3 “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–5) Starter — the social-media “brand cube”: what tells you which brand it is? Pupils TICK ALL brand cues (multi-select): logo shape, colours, symbol/letter.
   • Support: what is a logo? (choose).  • Core: a brand you know by colour.  • Challenge: why repeat the logo/colours on many products.
2. (5–18) Choose + recolour a logo (I do). Pick the eye/laptop/webcam logo; use ONLY the fill tool; Save As a new name. Play the recolour video as a hook. Likely error: forgets to rename → fix-words “Save As, new name.”
3. (18–35) Build a slide (we do). Set background colour (play the background-colour video); add the logo, charity name, key message; set font type/size/colour. Use a SIMPLE template so the design is theirs; reuse L2 colour codes.
4. (35–48) You-do — swap with a partner; add ONE positive comment + ONE improvement.  • Support: fill tool / comment = choose.  • Core: colour + message with reasons.  • Challenge: background + text colours that are easy to read. Pupils screenshot their slide.
5. (48–55) Plenary — self-tick the 3 “I can…”; show the slide and the partner’s comment.
Differentiation on the SAME task; minimal writing; videos are teacher-played hooks (motion/sound) — otherwise no flashing/sound.

lessons.title: Creating a brand — plan a style, make three slides
lessons.objectives:
I can plan a consistent layout for a set of slides
I can modify a logo so it fits the planned slide style
I can create a styled set of slides based on a plan
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on board: brand · logo. TA: graphics app, the blank three-slide set, the logo files, and the rubric where pupils can see it. Read the 3 “I can…” aloud. This lesson is STYLE, not content.
1. (0–5) Starter — branding or content? Pupils TICK ALL the branding parts (multi-select): logo, colours, shapes (NOT titles/text).
   • Support: branding stays the same (choose).  • Core: an example of content.  • Challenge: why branding should match on all three slides.
2. (5–12) The task (I do). A charity wants three blank branded slides with a unified look + a logo. Read the three missions: Our Identity · No to Bullies! · Age Appropriate; pupils pick one.
3. (12–25) Plan your style (we do). Choose logo, colours, font on the activity worksheet; check against the rubric. Colour tool again; black/white text is safe.
4. (25–35) Recolour the logo (you do). Open the chosen logo, recolour to the plan, Save As. Likely error: colours don’t match the slides → fix-words “use your plan’s main colour.”
5. (35–50) Make three slides (you do). Style the blanks to the plan; add the logo (same spot each time); add example text to set the font.  • Support: keep the logo in the same place.  • Core: one main colour across all three.  • Challenge: consistent shades. Pupils screenshot all three.
6. (50–55) Plenary — self-tick the 3 “I can…”; check work against the rubric.
Differentiation on the SAME task; minimal writing; no flashing/sound on slides.

lessons.title: Adding content — text, image, evaluate
lessons.objectives:
I can search for suitable text for slides
I can search for and add a suitable image
I can evaluate content against a rubric
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on board: content · licence · source. TA: a search engine + a policy-checked image site + slides software with comments; the rubric to hand. Read the 3 “I can…” aloud. Keep a BLANK copy of the styled slides.
1. (0–5) Starter — what would you change? The styled slides are empty. Pupils TICK ALL the content still needed (multi-select): name, key message, facts, a matching image.
   • Support: what is “content”? (choose).  • Core: one thing to add.  • Challenge: why no animations for a safety message.
2. (5–15) The task (I do). Slide 1 the charity name; slide 2 a key message; slide 3 two or three facts + a matching image. Show the content rows of the rubric.
3. (15–25) Find + write the text (we do). Search for facts; write them in OWN words; add a source for any statistic/quote. Likely error: copies word-for-word → fix-words “in your own words, then add the source.”  • Support: sentence starters.
4. (25–35) Find + add an image (you do). A licensed image that MATCHES the text; resize from a corner to keep proportions. “Appropriate” = age + theme + permission. Pupils screenshot their slides.
5. (35–48) Evaluate (you do). Review a partner’s slides with the rubric: start positive, be specific, suggest one improvement; reply to a comment if you disagree, with a reason.  • Challenge: own-words facts + three-way “appropriate” image.
6. (48–55) Plenary — self-tick the 3 “I can…”.
Differentiation on the SAME task; minimal writing; no flashing/sound on slides.

lessons.title: Presenting — plan, present, evaluate
lessons.objectives:
I can plan how to deliver a presentation
I can explain my work to others through a presentation
I can evaluate my work against a rubric
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on board: present · design choices. TA: the rubric and the planning rows on the worksheet; agree calm grouping for presenting. Read the 3 “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–5) Starter — a good presentation. Pupils TICK ALL the things a good presenter does (multi-select): speaks clearly, looks up, practises (NOT reads every word).
   • Support: a good presentation is simple/planned/practised (choose).  • Core: one thing a good presenter does with their voice.  • Challenge: why plan rather than read off the slide.
2. (5–18) Plan your talk (I do → we do). One sentence per slide; explain a design choice. Pupils fill the plan, then practise with a partner. Support: one sentence per slide.
3. (18–38) Present (you do). Present to a small group as if to the charity. Offer a CALM option (present to one peer or the TA) for anyone who finds the class too much; supervise.
4. (38–48) Evaluate (you do). Mark the rubric statements met — branding (blank slides) then content (example slides).  • Core: one design choice + why.  • Challenge: how the choices serve the safety message.
5. (48–55) Plenary — “working between applications”: name the apps used (browser, publishing, graphics, presentation) and how text/images moved (copy-paste, save-load). Self-tick the 3 “I can…”.
Differentiation on the SAME task; minimal writing; movement/regulation is routine; no flashing/sound on slides.
